questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most important first aim of providing first aid to an injured athlete on the sports field?

options.text: To preserve life and prevent worsening of the condition
To replace the role of a qualified medical doctor entirely
To carry out radiological imaging at the field
To prescribe oral antibiotics for the injury

questions.explanation: The three classic aims of first aid are to preserve life, prevent the condition from worsening and promote recovery; preservation of life through airway and circulation control is the highest priority before any other measure on the field.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: First aid supplements rather than replaces medical care.
Imaging requires hospital equipment and trained staff.
Antibiotic prescription is a medical, not first-aid, role.

questions.question_text: A Thomas splint is a piece of first-aid equipment used most appropriately for the immobilisation of which of the following body regions?

options.text: Forearm and wrist
Femur and lower limb
Cervical spine
Clavicle and shoulder

questions.explanation: The Thomas splint provides traction and immobilisation for fractures of the femur and the lower limb; it reduces pain, controls bleeding into the thigh and prevents further soft-tissue damage during transport to definitive care.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Forearm fractures use shorter padded splints, not Thomas.
Cervical injuries need a rigid collar and spinal board.
Clavicle injuries are typically managed with a sling.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate first-aid action for a young athlete who has fainted briefly on a hot training ground but is now responsive?

options.text: Make them sit upright in a chair without legs raised
Lay them flat with legs raised slightly above heart level
Pour cold water directly into the mouth forcefully
Encourage them to start sprinting immediately

questions.explanation: For a recovering fainter, lay them flat in a cool area with legs raised slightly above heart level to improve cerebral blood flow, loosen tight clothing and offer oral fluids in small sips once they are fully alert and able to swallow.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Sitting upright reduces cerebral blood flow further.
Forcing fluids risks aspiration in a recovering victim.
Sprinting after fainting risks repeat collapse.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the most appropriate immediate first-aid step for a player suffering a heavy nosebleed during a basketball match?

options.text: Tilt the head far backward and swallow the blood
Sit upright, lean head slightly forward and pinch the soft part of the nose
Pack the nostrils tightly with stones or sand
Stand up quickly and run laps around the court

questions.explanation: For epistaxis, the athlete should sit upright leaning slightly forward, breathe through the mouth, and pinch the soft cartilaginous part of the nose firmly for about ten minutes; this allows clot formation while preventing blood from being swallowed.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Tilting back makes blood flow into the throat and lungs.
Stones or sand cause infection and obstruction.
Running raises blood pressure and worsens bleeding.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate first-aid action for an athlete bitten by a snake during an outdoor cross-country event in a remote area?

options.text: Apply a tight tourniquet far above the bite site
Keep the limb still, immobilise it and arrange urgent transport
Cut the wound open and suck out the venom orally
Allow the casualty to walk vigorously to the vehicle

questions.explanation: Modern snakebite first aid involves keeping the limb still, applying a pressure-immobilisation bandage where appropriate, identifying the snake from a safe distance and arranging urgent transport to a hospital with antivenom; movement spreads venom systemically.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Tight tourniquets cause limb ischaemia without halting venom spread.
Cutting and oral suction risk infection and rescuer poisoning.
Vigorous movement actively pumps venom around the body.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best represents the correct first-aid response for a known asthmatic player who develops sudden wheezing and breathlessness after a long sprint?

options.text: Lay them completely flat and force water into mouth
Sit upright, calm them and help them use a reliever inhaler
Give them an unrelated cough syrup from another player
Push them to keep running to clear the airways

questions.explanation: Acute asthma first aid involves sitting the player upright in a calm environment, helping administration of a short-acting reliever (e.g. salbutamol) and calling for medical help if breathing does not improve; lying flat or further exertion worsens hypoxia.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Lying flat impairs lung expansion in an asthma attack.
Random medication from others can be harmful.
Continued running worsens bronchospasm and oxygen demand.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the standard initial first-aid action for an athlete with severe partial-thickness burns from a hot water spillage in the changing room?

options.text: Cool the burn with running tap water for 20 minutes and cover loosely
Apply toothpaste, oil or butter directly to the burn surface
Burst all blisters with a clean pin to drain fluid
Rub the burn with ice cubes for over an hour

questions.explanation: Cool a burn with running tepid tap water for at least 20 minutes within three hours of injury, remove constricting items, cover loosely with cling film or a sterile non-stick dressing and seek medical care; never apply household substances or ice.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Toothpaste and oil trap heat and increase infection risk.
Bursting blisters opens the wound to bacteria.
Direct ice causes additional cold tissue damage.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the safest first-aid response for a footballer who suffers a clear loss of consciousness after a head clash and is now confused on regaining awareness?

options.text: Allow him to immediately resume the match without assessment
Remove from play, monitor closely and refer for full concussion assessment
Give him alcohol to relax and reduce headache
Insist that he sleep alone at home with no monitoring

questions.explanation: Any loss of consciousness or confusion after head impact must trigger immediate removal from play under the 'when in doubt, sit them out' rule; the athlete needs medical assessment using SCAT and graduated return-to-play to prevent second-impact syndrome.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Returning to play exposes the brain to second-impact syndrome.
Alcohol masks symptoms and impairs neurological monitoring.
Unmonitored sleep can miss deteriorating intracranial bleeding.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the correct first-aid management of severe heatstroke in an outdoor athlete with very high body temperature and altered mental state?

options.text: Continue intense training to sweat more and lower temperature
Move to shade, cool aggressively with cold water immersion or wet sheets and call emergency services
Cover with thick blankets to maintain warmth
Give a hot energy drink to raise blood sugar levels

questions.explanation: Severe heatstroke is a medical emergency: move the athlete to shade, remove excess clothing, cool aggressively with cold water immersion or wet sheets and fanning, monitor airway and breathing, and call emergency services without delay.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Continuing training worsens hyperthermia rapidly.
Thick blankets trap heat dangerously.
Hot drinks add to heat load instead of removing it.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate first-aid action for a player choking on a piece of food during a half-time break, with no air movement and an inability to cough?

options.text: Encourage the player to keep eating more food
Give five back blows followed by five abdominal thrusts (Heimlich)
Pour water down the throat forcefully
Allow the player to lie flat and rest quietly

questions.explanation: For a fully obstructed conscious choking adult, alternate cycles of five firm back blows between the shoulder blades and five abdominal thrusts (Heimlich manoeuvre) until the obstruction clears or the casualty becomes unconscious, then commence CPR.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: More food worsens the airway obstruction.
Pouring water risks pushing food deeper into the airway.
Lying flat can cause complete airway blockage.

questions.question_text: Match each acute medical emergency in Column A with the most appropriate first-aid intervention listed in Column B and choose the correct combination from the options below.

column_a.text: Adult choking with no air movement

column_b.text: Sit upright and use reliever inhaler
Five back blows then five abdominal thrusts

questions.explanation: Asthma uses an upright posture and reliever inhaler; choking uses back blows and abdominal thrusts; conscious hypoglycaemia receives oral fast-acting glucose; anaphylaxis demands immediate intramuscular adrenaline auto-injector for life-saving effect.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Asthma is treated with bronchodilators, not adrenaline first.
Choking always uses back blows and abdominal thrusts.
Anaphylaxis requires immediate adrenaline injection.

questions.question_text: Match each first-aid item in Column A with the typical sports situation in which it would be used as listed in Column B and choose the correct combination.

column_b.text: Sling support for an injured arm

questions.explanation: A triangular bandage forms a sling for arm injuries; an adhesive plaster strip covers small superficial cuts; an ice pack wrapped in cloth controls acute swelling; sterile saline irrigates and cleans contaminated open wounds before dressing them.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Triangular bandages are best known for slings.
Adhesive strips are designed for small cuts.
Saline solutions are designed for wound irrigation.

questions.question_text: Match each component of the DRSABCD primary survey in Column A with its correct meaning listed in Column B and select the option showing the right mapping.

column_b.text: Check Danger and ensure scene safety
Check Response — speak and gently shake

questions.explanation: DRSABCD stands for Danger, Response, Send for help, Airway, Breathing, CPR (Compressions) and Defibrillation; this systematic primary survey ensures rescuer safety and prioritises lifesaving steps in a logical sequence.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: D specifically denotes checking for Danger first.
S calls for sending for help immediately.
ABC bundles airway, breathing and circulation steps.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason concerning concussion in sport and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in athlete safety.
questions.assertion: Any athlete suspected of concussion must be removed from play and not returned the same day even if symptoms resolve.
questions.reason: Returning to play with a recovering brain risks second-impact syndrome which can cause severe disability or death.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Same-day return to play after concussion exposes the recovering brain to a second impact, which can trigger catastrophic cerebral oedema (second-impact syndrome); modern sports protocols universally enforce same-day removal as the safest standard.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly explains the assertion's safety rule.
Both statements are codified in concussion guidelines.
Second-impact syndrome is well-documented clinically.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason about applying tourniquets in sports first aid and choose the option that best describes their logical connection.
questions.assertion: A tourniquet should be applied for any minor cut sustained by an athlete during a school-level match.
questions.reason: Tourniquets are reserved for life-threatening limb haemorrhage that cannot be controlled by direct pressure alone.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: The assertion is false because tourniquets are inappropriate for minor cuts and risk causing limb ischaemia and tissue damage; the reason is correct that tourniquets are reserved for catastrophic limb bleeding when direct pressure has failed.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Tourniquets are clearly inappropriate for minor cuts.
Direct pressure handles most bleeding effectively.
The reason states the correct indication for tourniquets.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason about CPR effectiveness on a collapsed athlete and choose the option that best describes their logical connection.
questions.assertion: Early bystander CPR significantly improves the chance of survival for an athlete who collapses with sudden cardiac arrest on the field.
questions.reason: Each minute without CPR after cardiac arrest reduces the chance of survival by approximately 7–10 percent.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Resuscitation studies show survival drops by about 7–10% per minute without CPR; bystander CPR maintains some cerebral and coronary perfusion until defibrillation, directly explaining the survival benefit of immediate compression-only or full CPR.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The 7–10% survival drop per minute is well-published.
Bystander CPR clearly improves outcomes in cardiac arrest.
The reason explains the survival mechanism correctly.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following on-field first-aid steps for an unconscious athlete with no breathing into the correct chronological order according to standard adult basic life support practice.

items.text: Open airway with head-tilt and chin-lift
Begin chest compressions at 100–120 per minute
Send a bystander to call emergency services and fetch an AED
Confirm scene safety and check responsiveness

options.text: S → R → P → Q
Q → P → R → S
P → S → Q → R
R → P → S → Q

questions.explanation: Standard BLS proceeds from confirming scene safety and checking responsiveness, to sending for help and an AED, to opening the airway and assessing breathing, and finally beginning chest compressions when no breathing is detected.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Scene safety always precedes any patient contact.
Calling for help should occur before opening the airway.
Compressions follow airway and breathing assessment.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following first-aid steps for managing a near-drowning casualty rescued from a swimming pool into the correct chronological order on the pool deck.

items.text: Check breathing and pulse, begin CPR if absent
Safely retrieve casualty and lay on a firm surface
Send a bystander for emergency services and AED

options.text: Q → R → S → P
P → S → Q → R
S → P → R → Q
R → Q → S → P

questions.explanation: Near-drowning management starts with safe retrieval onto a firm surface, sending for emergency services and AED, opening the airway and removing visible obstruction, and finally checking breathing and pulse, beginning CPR if either is absent.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Retrieval onto a firm surface enables effective CPR.
Calling for help precedes airway intervention.
Breathing and pulse check guide the need for CPR.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: A first-aider should always seek consent from a conscious adult casualty before providing any first-aid treatment to them.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Consent is a basic ethical and legal principle of first aid; the first-aider should introduce themselves, ask for permission and explain what they intend to do whenever the conscious adult casualty is able to understand and respond.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Consent protects autonomy of the conscious casualty.
First-aiders must explain intentions clearly.
Implied consent applies only to unconscious casualties.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram of the DRSABCD primary survey for an unconscious athlete; identify the very first action a rescuer must take before approaching the collapsed casualty on the field.

options.text: Check airway and breathing first of all
Check Danger — make sure the scene is safe
Begin chest compressions immediately on arrival
Apply automated external defibrillator first

questions.explanation: The 'D' in DRSABCD stands for Danger; the rescuer must first ensure that the scene is safe for themselves, the casualty and any bystanders before approaching, since an injured rescuer cannot help, and unsafe scenes can produce more victims.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Airway check follows scene safety, not before it.
Compressions are applied later in the sequence.
AED use comes after CPR is initiated.
